data.title: Politics And The Markets 05/23/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Credo Q4 Preview: Massive AI Gains Meet Valuation Constraints
data.description:
Since my last Credo Technology (NASDAQ:CRDO) analysis, the stock has gained 39% in price.

Credo is positioned at a critical juncture prior to its Q4 FY2025 earnings announcement in the first week of June. After being hailed for incredible growth underpinned by state-of-the-art Active Electrical Cable ("AEC") technology and in-house-developed SerDes IP, Credo has won over investors by capitalizing on booming demand for AI-focused data center deployments. Although the company possesses appealing strategic benefits, real valuation needs to be rebased against high valuation multiples, slowdown in growth, and customer concentration risk. My updated investment thesis is still optimistic, but with a new price target of $75-$80, instead of $90, within 12 months.

Exceptional Growth Against Structural Market Risks

Credo's recent business performance indicates exceptional growth in the high-speed interconnect segment, i.e., in AI server rack infrastructure. In Q3 FY2025, revenue surged aggressively by 154% year-over-year to a record $135 million, driven mainly by hyper-growth demand for Credo's AEC technology. Sequential revenue growth was at 87%, reflecting aggressively expanding penetration with hyperscalers building AI training clusters. Management has guided that the company will generate Q4 revenues of $155-$165 million, reflecting approximately 160% year-over-year growth, solidly establishing FY2025 as an inflection year.

But this terrific growth pattern is raising legitimate investor concern regarding sustainability. Although hyperscale AI infrastructure investment has fueled recent demand, indicators of potential market normalization are starting to emerge (DeepSeek-R1 (DEEPSEEK) was a critical indicator). Capex cycles of hyperscalers, traditionally volatile, represent significant risk if key customers (like Amazon (AMZN)) cut future procurement levels or simply decide to change suppliers for structural reasons. Credo's top line is heavily reliant on a few large hyperscalers; specifically two customers represented approximately 54% of FY2024 revenue, with the largest individual customer representing 39%. In Q3 FY2025, AWS accounted for approximately 86% of Credo's revenue. Additionally, Credo transacts primarily on purchase orders versus long-term contract commitments, compounding the vulnerability of a surprise change in customers' sourcing plans or shifts in budgets.

The market will also need to prepare for a relatively subdued growth rate in future quarters as cloud service providers near early completion of the hyperscaling phase of the AI infrastructure build-out. We're looking at about 60% revenue growth for FY2026, a steep decline from triple-digit FY2025 rates, but still very healthy. Investors will need to closely monitor hyperscaler capex plans set out on earnings calls and industry macro reports, since Credo has significant exposure to these dynamics. I expect AI infrastructure capex to continue to scale in the medium term from Big Tech companies, but growth in capex will inevitably slow down. This could lead to volatility and logical valuation re-ratings or, in an ideal sentiment environment, a smooth normalization of returns.

Margin Dynamics & Profitability Drivers

Credo's latest financial success is as much about revenue growth acceleration as it is about dazzling margin growth via operating leverage and cost controls. Q3 FY2025 non-GAAP gross margins of 63.8% were enough to drive substantial bottom-line leverage as operating expenses increased far less quickly than revenues. This helped to produce an operating margin of 31.4% and non-GAAP EPS of $0.25, several times last year's $0.04.

However, future margin growth faces real headwinds. Projected ramp-ups in R&D spend (specifically on next-generation SerDes and optical DSP products) may restrain incremental margin growth. Credo's management has explicitly signaled a sustaining strategy for reinvesting in R&D to maintain technological leadership, likely to moderate margin increments in future quarters. So, near-term profitability gains will increasingly rely on tactical scaling vs. substantial incremental margin expansion, rationally placing the attention of competent analysts on operational diligence and strategic execution. Sophisticated investors will thus have to critically evaluate management's observation regarding future trends of expense growth and capital deployment strategies from the Q4 earnings call-with the knowledge that any unanticipated increase in operating expenses will compress profitability and, as a result, valuation multiples.

Valuation Risks & Price Target Recalibration

Credo's shares currently sell for about $61 per share, with forward multiples well above semiconductor industry norms. The stock is trading at 52 times FY2026 consensus EPS estimates of $1.15-$1.20 (estimates which align with my own conservative outlook). This premium valuation inherently includes high assumptions of sustained strong earnings growth, which places heavy execution pressure on Credo to repeatedly outperform market estimates. Such lofty multiples are only sustainable as long as Credo maintains high revenue growth, operating discipline, and technological superiority.

According to reasonable assumptions of growth moderation, my updated valuation analysis comes with cautions. With conservative scenario analysis, a non-GAAP TTM earnings multiple of 65-70 times FY2026 normalized EPS seems more plausible than the current EPS multiple. This backs my new 12-month price target range of $75-$80. However, investors need to realize the inherent sensitivity of models at such high multiples. Any seeming disappointment in quarterly earnings or guidance (i.e., disappointing weaker-than-expected Q4 results or conservative forward revenue guidance) potentially has the ability to trigger swift multiple compression and disproportionate volatility.

Therefore, investors must assess Credo with analytical restraint, watching quarterly figures intently and comparing these to Street targets for sentiment considerations. Personally, I sold out my small position to raise cash after a recent ~30% return in a few months. I have kept all of my other AI investments intact.

Strategic Catalysts & Competitive Positioning

Even with valuation headwinds, Credo has significant growth drivers that can continue to create long-term shareholder value, including a reasonably high probability of upward analyst estimate revisions and raised guidance from management. Product innovation is healthy, evidenced by recent releases of Lark optical DSP chips and next-generation PCIe/CXL retimers. Successful adoption in these leading-edge solutions by broader customer bases would de-risk customer concentration, enhance Credo's competitive advantage, and potentially extend total addressable markets. Commentary from management during the Q4 earnings call about pipeline growth, design-win traction, and strategy will provide critical information on Credo's continued efforts to differentiate itself in the market.

On the other hand, there are also continuing competitive threats, especially from more established semiconductor incumbents with large war chests. New optical interconnect technologies or hyperscaler internal developments may disrupt the company's existing market leadership. Investors need to stay aware of technological developments and competitive evolutions, underscoring the critical necessity of continued R&D spending and product innovation.

Strategic Portfolio Implications & Risk Management Synopsis

Healthy growth potential continues to be attractive, especially with Credo continuing to dominate specialized interconnect markets with better products. However, prudent stock portfolio management will require cautious sizing and close monitoring, considering concentrated customer risk, high valuation multiples, and some macroeconomic risk factors-like geopolitical tension involving Taiwan-based manufacturing partner TSMC (TSM).

Conclusion: Buy

Credo begins its Q4 FY2025 earnings report positioned between record past performance and impending future challenges requiring advanced analytical rigor to safeguard and time returns. My outlook is moderately hopeful based on sustained technological breakthroughs, market leadership in a secular growth environment, and cautious operational execution tempered wisely by valuation concerns and customer concentration risks.

My new 12-month target range of $75-$80 is a reflection of this optimistic conservatism, providing investors with modest upside while recognizing valuation risk. Portfolio managers should closely monitor subsequent Q4 results, managerial guidance, and general market indicators to weight Credo at a strategic percentage in risk-adjusted tech portfolios aggressively exposed to 2026 macroeconomic tailwinds.

data.title: KKR Has Less Market Exposure Than Feared, Making Shares Attractive
data.description:
Shares of KKR (NYSE:KKR) have had a roller coaster ride over the past year, rallying to record highs, only to drop 45% from those highs and subsequently rallying more than 25% from the lows. While its underlying business has not been as volatile, its long-term earnings do have market and macro sensitivity. I would however argue that the market is overstating this sensitivity. I last covered shares of KKR in March, upgrading the stock to a “buy.” Shares have been volatile since then, plunging after “Liberation Day” and then strongly rebounding, which has left them essentially flat, in-line with the market. Given updated financials and new macroeconomic uncertainties, now is a good time to revisit KKR.

KKR is of course a leading private equity giant, and private equity valuations will generally be correlated to equity market levels. As such, KKR’s management fees will have sensitivity to overall market levels. Plus, any performance fees it earns will have significant beta to market levels. However, I think it is important to emphasize that KKR gets the majority of its earnings from regular fees and not performance results. As you can see, investment earnings account for just about 20% of its earnings power.

This 20% share would likely fall significantly in a downturn, but baseline fee revenue should hold in relatively well. Of course, with the market having rebounded from its lows, the worst fears that weighed on the stock in early April are also less relevant. KKR is also very well insulated from panic selling and fund outflows. That is because most of its AUM has significant lock-up periods. 42% of capital is perpetual, given its ownership of the Global Atlantic insurance company, and most other funds have an 8-year lockup period. This structure provides significant stability to its asset base.

Much of these strengths can be seen in the company’s recent results. In the company’s first quarter, KKR reported a GAAP loss of $0.22. These results were impacted by $1.4 billion of net investment losses in its insurance subsidiary. Insurance companies are largely hold-to-maturity investors, so these mark-to-market movements can be adjusted out, which would have left EPS closer to $1.15 in adjusted net income. It generated $1.1 billion of operating earnings, or $1.24 per share, up 16% from last year.

As an asset manager, the most important thing to generate long-term earnings growth is to accumulate more assets on which it can generate fees. KKR is doing this very well. It raised $31 billion in Q1, flat with last year. Over the past year, it has raised $114 billion. Thanks to net new assets and market appreciation, KKR now has $664 billion of assets, up 15% from last year.

Fee paying AUM is lagging total AUM by $138 billion even as it rose 12%; this gap is up from $107 billion a year ago. Perpetual capital grew by 16% and is now 52% of fee-paying AUM. As it invests capital and makes more AUM fee-paying, earnings will mechanistically rise. KKR has $64 billion of committed capital that will begin paying fees when invested. This will generate about $640 million of incremental cash flow.

Plus, KKR has $116 billion in uncalled commitments, up $18 billion from a year ago. As it finds attractive opportunities, it can call in these commitments to grow the business. Essentially, we have high visibility that KKR should grow by 10-15%, just by taking advantage of money it has already raised but has yet to deploy or earn fees on. Importantly, KKR is deploying capital. It invested $19 billion in the quarter and has another $13 billion already lined up for Q2.

Of course, we also want to see KKR winning new business and not just relying on past wins to grow. Essential to that is generating strong investment returns. With solidly positive returns over the past year, KKR is well positioned to keep accumulating assets. With easier regulatory burdens, there are also new channels for growth. Most notably, in April, it launched two fixed income products targeting individual investors with plans for two equity strategies to be launched next year. While I expect these to start slowly, tapping the private wealth market greatly increases KKR’s addressable market.

With these favorable fundamental dynamics, KKR generated $823 million of fee related earnings, or $0.92 per share, which was up 23% from last year. Management fees grew by just over $100 million to $917 million while fee-related compensation rose by 22% to $210 million. Fee performance revenues were up over 10% to $21 million, an extremely small share, pointing to the relative stability of fee revenue.

It is also important to note that while private equity is a critical part of the business, KKR Is no longer primarily a PE firm. Private equity accounts for $209 billion of assets and $354 million of fee revenue. Credit and liquid strategies are actually the biggest share of AUM, at $284 billion. They generate $325 million in fee revenue, a bit lower than private equity despite a higher AUM as credit strategies generally have lower fee rates, given lower return targets. Credit strategies rose $14 billion of capital in the quarter, accounting for nearly half of new business and are likely to continue to be a larger share of assets over time. Real assets is the smallest segment with $171 billion of assets, accounting for $292 million in fee revenue. I expect fee revenue to continue growing double digits given AUM growth.

Elsewhere, insurance net investment income rose to $1.7 billion, up about $240 million, given inflows and higher yields. The net cost of insurance also rose by about $240 million to $1.2 billion offsetting these gains, as lower-cost legacy policies run off. G&A costs rose by $19 million to $229 million, given growth. With net investment income failing to outrun insurance costs and G&A rising, insurance earnings fell by $14 million to $259 million. With ongoing AUM growth but higher funding costs, I expect insurance profits to be generally flat over the next 12-18 months. I would note that insurance book value grew by $1.4 billion to $9.5 billion.

Looking to more volatile components, investment earnings were $273 million, including $88 million of performance income. KKR has $398 billion of performance fee eligible AUM, up 16% from last year. Again, I would emphasize that performance income is less than $0.10 of EPS. Even in a downturn if this were to be eliminated, it is a manageable earnings headwind but not a catastrophic one. This is why I believe investors can overstate KKR’s cyclicality. One positive for investment earnings is that it now appears unlikely the tax treatment on carried interest will change in this year’s tax bill. $245 billion of AUM is above cost and earning carry.

Elsewhere, Strategic Holdings grew by 50% to $31 million. As I noted in March, KKR increased its stake in several Strategic Holdings businesses, and it share of EBITDA is now $920 million. It expects $350 million of operating earnings next year with that eventually growing to $1.1 billion by 2030. As you can see, these holdings are a diversified mix of businesses. I continue to view these growth ambitions as extremely aggressive, and my own valuation estimate assumes KKR falls short here.

Finally, I would note that KKR has an excellent balance sheet with $7.1 billion of cash and $8.6 billion of debt, for just $1.5 billion of net debt. It pays just a modest 0.6% dividend yield. I do expect KKR to use retained free cash flow to grow its investments rather than return capital to shareholders.

Back in March, I reduced my target multiple to 16-17x from 17-18x. On $8 of forward EPS, I had a $132 price target. With economic uncertainty certainly persisting, I do expect multiple compression to persist. I continue to think ~20x fee income and ~10x investment income is the right multiple, pointing to ~18x, but I believe over the next 6-12 months, valuation is likely to remain short of this threshold. With ongoing AUM growth and capital deployment, I now see $8-$8.50 in earnings next year so long as the S&P stays above ~5,800. As such, I see upside towards $135, creating a double-digit return opportunity that makes KKR an attractive buy for investors.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: NGS Offerings To Be Boosted By Moves In AI Space
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:PANW), published in February 2025, I previewed the company’s Q2 earnings results and discussed the key factors that investors need to watch out for in the company’s Q2 report. I had a HOLD rating on the stock.

Since the article was published, PANW’s stock has declined 7.8%, underperforming the S&P 500, which has declined 3.3% during the same period.

In this article, I analyse the company’s Q3 report and discuss the key takeaways, especially with respect to the company’s platformization strategy and its Next Generation Security Offerings [NGS].

Third Quarter Highlights

It was a decent quarter from PANW, with a beat on both the top- and bottom-lines. More specifically, Q3 revenues came in at $2.29 billion, up 15.33% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $8.99 million. Non-GAAP diluted EPS came in at $0.80, up 21.2% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $0.03. Non-GAAP operating margins came in at 27.4%. Remaining Performance Obligations (RPOs) also came in strong, jumping 19% y/y to $13.5 billion.

The company also issued a strong guidance for both Q4 and for the overall year. More specifically, FY25 revenues are expected to come in between $9.17 billion and $9.19 billion, with the mid-point being in-line with analyst estimates. FY25 Non-GAAP diluted EPS is expected to come in between $3.26 and $3.28, with the mid-point beating analyst estimates by $0.04. FY25 RPOs are expected to come in between $15.2 and $15.3 billion.

For Q4 specifically, revenues are expected to come in between $2.49 billion and $2.51 billion, with the mid-point of the guidance being in-line with the estimate. Non-GAAP Diluted EPS is projected to come in between $0.87 and $0.89, with the mid-point of the guidance marginally beating analyst estimates by $0.01.

Finally, management announced that they would be opportunistic with respect to share buybacks and that they still have $1 billion remaining to issue buybacks.

The $15 Billion ARR Goal Remains on Track Thanks to Shrewd Moves in AI Cybersecurity

One of the key announcements that PANW has made in recent times, one that has been referenced repeatedly throughout FY25, is the company’s target of reaching $15 billion in Annual Recurring Revenue [ARR] by 2030. One of the catalysts that the company is betting on to reach this target has been its Next Generation Security [NGS] Offerings, which includes the likes of Prisma Cloud and Cortex.

If one looks at the progress of this metric, then one should have the confidence that the company remains on track to hit the $15 billion target. While the y/y growth of NGS ARR has not followed a straight line, in absolute terms, the NGS ARR has been increasing steadily with each passing quarter. Q3 was the first quarter when this metric crossed the $5 billion milestone, and based on Q4 guidance, $6 billion is not that far off.

One of the key factors behind the steady growth of this metric has to do with the AI revolution. As more and more companies utilize AI at workplace, AI cybersecurity has become even more critical. This urgency has aided PANW’s growth, especially given the demand seen for XSIAM, the company’s AI-driven security operations platform. XSIAM saw a more than 200% y/y growth in its ARR. XSIAM was a major catalyst behind the company’s overall ARR growth.

In line with the strong demand for AI-related cybersecurity offerings, the company also announced a “comprehensive AI security platform”, called Prisma AIRS. In addition, the company also announced that it would be acquiring Protect AI. Protect AI is an expert in dealing with cybersecurity applications specifically in the AI and Machine Learning (ML) domains. Given that the company is already a leader in the AI security space, and given that it has formed partnerships with the likes of MSFT and Databricks, PANW is acquiring a company that should allow it to accelerate its platformization strategy at a faster pace than before, given that the AI cybersecurity market is expected to grow substantially in the coming years.

With respect to the company’s platformization strategy, Q3 offered more evidence that the decision to pivot to this strategy last year continues to pay off. The number of platformization deals has been steadily increasing with each passing quarter. The company’s NGS offerings have played a major role in aiding this growth, and with PRISMA AIRS and the acquisition of Protect AI, the AI cybersecurity platform that the company is planning to offer should accelerate the overall growth of platformization strategy.

Overall, on-the-face of it, investors may have felt that PANW’s quarter was nothing special, and this was evidenced by the declining stock price post the earnings release. But when one digs deeper, then there is enough evidence to suggest that the company remains on track to achieve its long-term goals. And no matter how challenging the macroenvironment may be, given the AI revolution, the cybersecurity space has become more important than ever. With PRISMA AIRS and the acquisition of Protect AI, PANW has laid the foundation, in my opinion, to not only achieve its goals but also to remain well-positioned to be a global leader in this space for a long time.

Valuation

Forward P/E Approach

Price Target

$230.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

54.8x

Projected FY25 EPS

$3.35

Projected Forward PEG Ratio

2.16

Projected FY26 EPS Growth

25.4%

Projected FY26 EPS

$4.20

Source: Company’s Q3FY25 Press Release, LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), Seeking Alpha and Author’s Calculations

As mentioned earlier, the company now expects FY25 diluted non-GAAP EPS to come be in the range between $3.26 and $3.28. As I mentioned in one of my articles on PANW, the company has consistently exceeded its high-end of the guidance by an average of 2.1%. I have assumed that the company would beat FY25 guidance by this magnitude, which would result in a projected FY25 EPS of $3.35. This would imply that Q4 diluted EPS would need to come in $0.96, $0.09 higher than consensus estimate. The company has beaten the consensus by this margin before and as such, this is a reasonable estimate in my opinion. The new assumption is higher than my previous estimate of $3.22 (on a split-adjusted basis).

The company, according to LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), currently trades at a forward P/E of 51.1x, lower than its 5-year historical median multiple of 52.1x. PANW’s long-term average EPS growth, according to LSEG Data, stands at 13.56%. Based on my projected FY25 EPS, EPS growth would be 18%, higher than the long-term average. As such, in my opinion, PANW deserves a multiple higher than its historical median multiple. In my last article, I had given the company a multiple of 54.8x, which is what it was trading at the time. Given the EPS growth, I have maintained this multiple for my calculations.

According to Seeking Alpha, PANW currently trades a forward PEG ratio of 2.70, higher than both the sector median (1.67) and its 5-year average (2.39). The table below shows the different price targets based on these different PEG ratios. The forward P/E multiple remains at 54.8x and the FY25 projected EPS remains at $3.35 for all three scenarios.

The average price target for PANW based on the three scenarios comes to $230, which represents an upside of about 27% from current levels. The company, according to Seeking Alpha, also has a decent momentum, with an overall and a short-term momentum grade of B- on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum scale. Taking things together, I am upgrading my rating on the stock from a HOLD to a BUY.

Risk Factors

While PANW management, during the earnings call, assured investors that the company itself remains immune from any tariffs, the same cannot be said about its clients. Moreover, one of the reasons why the company couldn’t maximize the quarter was because of tariff-related disruptions its clients faced in April. The tariff-related uncertainties aren’t going anywhere, and should the disruptions continue, or get worse, then the company would face an indirect impact via its clients. This would once again, not allow the company to maximize its potential, thereby affecting my bull case.

The other risk factor that investors should consider, in my opinion, would be if the acquisition of Protect AI doesn’t pan out as expected. During the earnings call, CEO Nikesh Arora mentioned that the rationale behind this acquisition was to enhance its AI portfolio by integrating AI runtime security. Mr. Arora mentioned that the acquisition fast-tracked this process by saving the company about “six months to build it.” Should the synergies fail to materialize, then it would have a negative impact on the company’s AI offerings, which would subsequently negatively impact my bull case.

Concluding Thoughts

It was a decent quarter from PANW, with a marginal beat on both the top- and bottom-lines. However, its NGS ARR really stood out, crossing the $5 billion milestone. Furthermore, the company also announced PRISMA AIRS, a comprehensive AI cybersecurity platform, as well as its intent to acquire Protect AI, a leader in AI cybersecurity space. The new offering along with the acquisition should enable the company to further grow its platformization strategy, which remains on a solid footing. At the same time, the company has laid the groundwork to ensure that it achieves its long-term goal of $15 billion in ARR by 2030.

From a valuation perspective, there remains considerable upside from current levels. As such, I am upgrading my rating on the stock from a HOLD to a BUY.

The AI revolution continues to chug along even when the macroenvironment remains challenging. As such cybersecurity has taken on even more importance. Based on the moves made in Q3, PANW remains well-positioned to capitalize on this trend.

data.title: FRTY: A Focused Mid Cap Growth ETF Throwing Caution To The Wind
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Alger Mid Cap 40 ETF (NYSEARCA:FRTY) is a highly aggressive and concentrated mid-cap growth ETF comprised mainly of speculative names trading at excessive valuations. FRTY's 0.60% expense ratio (1.14% before waivers) doesn't help shareholders on the cost front, and it's certainly not justified, given how FRTY has been the worst-performing fund in its category since its launch four years ago. For me, this is an easy "sell," but I will highlight its components' growth potential in the article below and compare FRTY with some more reasonably-priced mid-cap growth alternatives. I hope you enjoy the read.

FRTY Overview

Strategy Discussion

According to its fact sheet, FRTY is driven by the following philosophy:

Alger has been committed to a growth style of investing since the firm was founded in 1964. We believe that the best way to uncover and evaluate such companies is through intensive, fundamental, proprietary investment research. At Alger, we believe companies undergoing Positive Dynamic Change offer the best investment opportunities. By Positive Dynamic Change, we mean those companies experiencing High Unit Volume Growth and Positive Life Cycle Change.

FRTY's prospectus describes Positive Life Cycle Change as "companies benefitting from new regulations, a new product innovation or new management" and clarifies that the manager's selection universe is generally the Russell MidCap Growth Index or the S&P MidCap 400 Index. In addition, a Flyer describes Alger's unique investment approach that relies on bottom-up research to select high-quality stocks with strong growth potential. As the ETF's name implies, FRTY targets 40 stocks, which allows the manager to achieve a high active share with its benchmark. As shown below, FRTY's active share was 78.58%, which implies only a 21.42% overlap with the Russell MidCap Growth Index.

As expected, this table also reveals strong sales and earnings growth characteristics, but its 35.43x P/E ratio is abnormally high. On the other hand, its P/B ratio is much lower, but that valuation metric isn't very useful for growth stocks, as it doesn't capture intangible assets, which can be quite significant. Instead, the takeaway is that FRTY is richly valued, and it's doubtful that managers will consider that factor at all. I offer this as the primary reason for FRTY's significant underperformance, as detailed next.

Performance Summary

In this article, I will compare FRTY against the following four ETFs:

  1. iShares Russell Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IWP)
  2. Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth ETF (VOT)
  3. iShares Morningstar Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IMCG)
  4. Janus Henderson Mid Cap Growth Alpha ETF (JMID)

IWP represents FRTY's benchmark, while VOT and IMCG are the types of broad-based passive funds FRTY aims to beat. Lastly, I included JMID because it's a new ETF with some strong fundamentals. It's less concentrated than FRTY, with 80 holdings, but its active share with IWP is reasonably low at 39% compared to 25% for FRTY as of May 21, 2025.

Unfortunately, FRTY has delivered a -1.39% annualized return since April 2021, lagging substantially behind IWP, VOT, and IMCG. As you might expect from an aggressive growth fund, its volatility (standard deviation) is elevated, and its range of returns (e.g., best vs. worst year) is extreme.

To succeed, FRTY needs to hit that 38.27% "best year" figure more often and find a way to avoid 50% drawdowns like what it experienced between November 2021 and December 2022. Its active returns compared to IWP provides greater perspective on this - we see it outperformed by 17.01% in 2024, but trailed by 7-15% per year in the other four years.

Consider how even if FRTY matched its 17.01% outperformance from 2024, it would still be about 22% behind IWP cumulatively, and that's because it's so hard to recover from huge negative years. For example, to break even after 2022's 42.23% decline, the ETF requires a 73.10% gain, and those types of gains are rare even in strong bull markets. Therefore, the takeaway is that Alger is doing its shareholders no favors by ignoring risk. There are several ways to do this (e.g., low standard deviation, low valuation, low concentration, high quality), and we know that FRTY at least ignores the first three. However, Alger's claims of seeking "high-quality" stocks are questionable. To see what I mean, let's dig deeper into the fund's composition and fundamentals.

FRTY Analysis

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between FRTY, IWP, VOT, IMCG, and JMID as of May 20, 2025. It reveals how FRTY overweights Technology and Communication Services stocks by 5.60% and 8.86% on average, with the main offset in Financials. It's also normal to see a somewhat high allocation to the Health Care sector, which has a nice mix of stable companies and innovative ones with breakthrough technologies.

The fund's website lists its top ten as of April 30, 2025, and they include Palantir Technologies (PLTR) and GFL Environmental (GFL). However, I noticed GFL was dropped sometime in May, and the ETF is currently 8.85% in cash. Based on a disclaimer, I believe cash was purposely omitted from the holdings list below and in the fund's fact sheet.

I initially thought the high cash allocation was an anomaly, but after reviewing historical holdings list and the fund's latest annual and semi-annual reports, it's actually quite common. The semi-annual report noted a 10.30% allocation to cash on June 30, 2024, and not being fully invested raises questions about the confidence FRTY's manager has about her team's selections. I assume it's kept this high for liquidity reasons, but I couldn't find an explanation on the Alger website.

Fundamental Analysis: Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlight selected fundamental metrics for FRTY's top 25 holdings, which comprise 81.99% on a cash-adjusted basis. As discussed earlier, this concentration level is high, but that's also the point. Alger believes that to produce returns that are different from a benchmark, they have to be willing to deviate from that benchmark. Put differently, there's definitely no "passive indexing" going on with FRTY.

Here are four additional takeaways to consider:

FRTY: High Risk, Questionable Quality

FRTY currently has a 1.60 five-year beta, indicating that it's substantially more volatile than the broader market and other mid-cap growth ETFs. Alger likely views this feature positively, as high-beta portfolios often outperform in bull markets. However, it's not that simple, and the downside risk of some of these holdings is huge. For example, consider the following 2022 total returns for some of FRTY's top holdings:

  • Palantir Technologies (PLTR): -64.74%
  • AppLovin (APP): -88.83%
  • Repligen Corp. (RGEN): -36.07%
  • Cloudfare (NET): -65.62%
  • Vertiv Holdings (VRT): -45.25%
  • Sea Ltd. (SE): -76.74%
  • Natera (NTRA): -56.99%

Investors have to be prepared for these types of losses again, and as I discussed earlier, they'll require an even bigger gain just to break even. I'm also not convinced Alger does a good job selecting "quality" companies. As shown in the last column of the table, FRTY's 15.07% weighted average ROE is 4% lower than IMCG and a massive 19% behind JMID. With 10/40 holdings not profitable over the last year, readers can add "questionable quality" to the list of reasons why markets will likely punish these stocks during the next market downturn.

FRTY: An Obvious Momentum Play

FRTY is down by 5.14% YTD, but the table shows that on a weighted average basis, its current holdings are up by 16.48%, the highest in the peer group. You might be wondering why there is a discrepancy, and the simple answer is that FRTY did not hold all these stocks before they skyrocketed. In addition, you don't see the stocks FRTY held at the end of 2024 that have significantly declined this year (e.g., ALAB, TTD, ILMN, CAVA, GLBE), and with a portfolio turnover rate that's ranged from 199-417% over the last four years, it's not difficult to see what's happening. FRTY is not a high-conviction ETF, but rather, the Alger team focuses on stocks with strong short/mid-term momentum features and hopes they can "ride the wave" up, so to speak.

In my view, the strategy is pretty basic, and those interested may want to check out the Invesco S&P MidCap Momentum ETF (XMMO). XMMO matched FRTY's "best year" return, but its maximum drawdown was 27% better, and you can see the impact that's had on total returns since April 2021.

FRTY: Extreme Valuations

FRTY has a 36.56x forward P/E ratio compared to 28.39x for IWP, which represents a 29% premium over its benchmark. I found these significant premiums using other measures of valuation, including trailing EV-EBITDA (32.71% vs. 21.44x) and trailing Price-Sales (5.96x vs. 2.54x), so it's pretty clear valuation is not considered by FRTY's team of analysts.

The table above shows how JMID easily has the best value features, evidenced in part by a 20.77x forward P/E ratio that's 57% cheaper than FRTY. The three broad-based funds (IWP, VOT, and IMCG) trade at forward P/E's that are 6-10 points cheaper, and cheaper valuations should provide these ETFs with better downside protection in market downturns.

FRTY: Outstanding Growth Potential

FRTY's growth prospects are outstanding, with most components having at least 20%+ expected EBITDA growth over the next twelve months. As a whole, FRTY's weighted average one-year estimate EBITDA growth rate is 28.87%, which is about 7% more than IWP and 10-14% above the other three ETFs listed. I also noticed that historical sales growth is trending "the right way." For example, the table below shows that FRTY's one-year sales growth rate is better than its three- and five-year sales growth rates (25.34% vs. 20.59% / 25.10%).

Those statistics place FRTY in about the top 5% of all U.S. Size & Style ETFs, and its constituents also delivered a solid 1.86% sales surprise last quarter, better than all peers listed above except JMID. Either way, FRTY's growth features are strong, but that's really the only reason to consider it.

Investment Recommendation

FRTY is a mid-cap growth ETF with too many negative features for my liking. Its 0.60% expense ratio (1.14% before waivers) makes it too costly to own, its valuation ratios are substantially above its peers, and its mix of high risk and questionable quality indicates it won't do well in market downturns. The strategy seems built around selecting high-growth stocks with strong short-term momentum features, but the team at Alger has yet to demonstrate an ability to select these stocks before their significant gains, so I don't believe it's an appropriate investment. Therefore, I've assigned FRTY a "sell" rating, and I look forward to hearing your thoughts in the comment section below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Kosmos Energy: Dirt Cheap But Buy At Your Own Peril (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

Kosmos Energy Ltd. (NYSE:KOS) stock has been crushed. That is no exaggeration as 52 week losses near 75%. While I believe the stock is dirt cheap, I also believe buying a struggling business may not be such a good idea. In the below analysis, I determine that the technicals look highly bearish as the stock has struggled to rebound with the market. As for the fundamentals, Q1 earnings were ugly but Q2 production guidance was acceptable. Even though revenue growth is far below average currently, it hasn't been extraordinary bad relative to the past few years. While that is the case, the P/S ratio has contracted heavily and so there is some disconnect between the valuation and the fundamentals. However, it has been said countless times that a buying a weak company at a cheap price may not be a great idea and so the fundamental setup is only neutral at best. As a result, I initiate Kosmos at a sell rating on the basis of likely continued technical weakness.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

Kosmos remains in downtrend as the trend line most recently acted as resistance back in late February. While many other stocks have broken their downtrend lines recently and have started new uptrends, this is not the case for Kosmos. This indicates poor strength as investors are obviously hesitant to buy the dip in this stock.

Support

There is only one support level for Kosmos in this timeframe. It is at 1.5, a potential key round number. This level has been a floor for the stock since early April and so it has the potential to act as support once again in the near future. To find more support levels, you would have to zoom out to a multiyear chart.

Resistance

In addition to the downtrend line, there are plenty of other resistance levels above the stock. The first is at 1.89 as it represented a ceiling back in April. The next resistance level is at about 2.1. This area was support in early March while being resistance just a few trading days ago. Moving up, 2.2 may be another important area as an early April gap down happened there on high volume. Lastly, there is relatively distant resistance in the low 2.6s as that level was support in December and late February.

Takeaway

I believe it is clear that the chart is a bearish one for Kosmos. The stock remains in a long term downtrend and there is far more resistance than there is support. Do note that many stocks that I recently covered have broken their downtrends as the market recovered. The lack of a recovery here shows that Kosmos is severely lacking strength.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average [SMA]

The 50 day SMA has been below the 200 day SMA for the past six months to show that long term bearishness has been present the entire time. The gap between the SMAs remains large but the trajectory of the 50 day SMA has improved slightly. Therefore, the gap between the lines has decreased modestly to show that downward momentum has slowed. Recently, the stock failed to break above the 50 day SMA as another sign of weakness.

Exponential Moving Average [EMA]

After a very brief bullish period, the 9 day EMA has crossed back below the 21 day EMA as a bearish near term signal. This isn't surprising given that the stock had broken back below both lines a few days ago. Moving forward, these EMAs may act as resistance. Note that the EMAs didn't even hold as support once during that brief bullish period.

Bollinger Bands

Earlier this month, Kosmos was able to break above the Bollinger Bands midline to show at least there is some near term reprieve from the long term downtrend. It actually broke above the upper band. While an overbought consolidation is therefore not surprising, the fact that the stock has broken back beneath the midline shows that the downtrend is regaining strength.

Takeaway

I would say that the moving averages were mainly negative in their signalling. The large gap between the SMAs shows that long term bearish momentum remains very strong, the EMAs just had a bearish crossover to show near term weakness, and the stock breaking back below the Bollinger Bands midline shows that the near term relief is likely over. The downtrend is strengthening again.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence [MACD]

The MACD had a bullish crossover back in mid April and after getting some traction earlier this month, the indicator line has fallen dangerously close to the signal line. A bearish crossover seems imminent after four red histogram bars. The MACD actually shows some positive divergence as the April low in the indicator seems to have held above the March low. This could be a potential bottoming signal.

Relative Strength Index [RSI]

Unlike the MACD, the RSI does not show positive divergence as its April low was in fact a six month low. Therefore, this offsets the bottoming signal with a confirmation of the stock's declines. After a recovery in the RSI, the indicator has fallen back below 50 to a current reading of 44.86. This shows that the bears have returned to power by a moderate margin.

Stochastics

Despite the rebound being weak, the stochastics had a bullish crossover earlier this month and both lines briefly entered the 80+ zone. A bearish crossover occurred not long after and now both lines are back below the 50 level as another indication of resurgent bears. On a brighter note, this indicator hasn't been significantly below the 20 level for some time and so the bears also haven't been overly dominant.

Takeaway

Negativity continues into Kosmos stock's indicators in my view. The MACD is nearing a bearish crossover after four red histogram bars, the RSI has dropped back below 50 to show bearish control, and the stochastics displayed multiple signals of weakness. Furthermore, the MACD's positive divergence signal was offset by the RSI's confirmation of the stock's plunge and so it would have been very risky to call the bottom.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Kosmos reported their 2025 Q1 earnings back on May 6th and showed concerning financial results. In the quarter, they produced around 60,500 boepd while sales were about 49,600 boepd. This generated revenues of $290 million, down 30.77% from $419 million in the prior year period. This also underperformed expectations as they missed by $28.73 million, showing that results were worse than feared. As shown above, revenue growth is far below average and also deteriorated from 2024 Q4. For EPS, they reported a diluted figure of -$0.23, missing expectations by a wide margin of $0.15. This is also a major weakening from the year ago quarter's +$0.19 figure. Also shown above is FCFs. In the past few years, positive FCFs have been unsustainable and now the figure has dropped significantly back into negative territory after a better Q4. Do note they stated that FCF was impacted by the "timing of liftings, heavy scheduled maintenance across the portfolio, and no cash flow contribution from GTA sales in the first quarter." Therefore, FCF results may not be as bad as they seem. As for the outlook for their 2025 Q2, the company is expecting to produce between 66,000-72,000 boepd. At the midpoint this implies that production will increase by 11% from 2024 Q2's 62,100 boepd figure. Therefore, at least their operations are expected to expand.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has shown major contraction from late 2023 as it has declined from 2.4 all the way down to a current reading of only 0.518. While the fundamentals don't look pretty, I believe this has been an overreaction. As discussed above, revenues were down over 30% YoY for their Q1 and the revenue growth rate is significantly below the multiyear average. Despite this, growth is actually still holding above the 2023 Q2 trough. Meanwhile, the P/S ratio has been punished continuously and has been below early to mid 2023 levels since the middle of last year. Therefore, there likely has been too much pessimism in the stock. As of this writing, the median P/S ratio for the Energy sector is 1.24 and so Kosmos is at a 58% discount to peers. While I won't say that isn't justified, with their Q2 production outlook being acceptable, the stock does look overall cheap at current levels.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, it was determined that the technicals show a bearish outlook for Kosmos. While the market has rebounded from its early April lows, the stock has been sluggish and has shown weakness overall. For the fundamentals, Q1 earnings were concerning with revenues, EPS, and FCF all dropping significantly. Production guidance for Q2 does show expected YoY growth, however. As discussed above, while the fundamentals are undesirable, the P/S ratio has contracted out of proportion, making the stock undervalued at current levels. However, buying a struggling business at a rock-bottom price usually doesn't payoff as much as it promises and so I would rate the fundamental setup as only neutral at this point. Therefore, I believe Kosmos is a sell with the technicals pointing towards further losses.

data.title: Viper Energy: Recent Transactions Increase Its Oil Production By Over 60%
data.description:
Viper Energy (NASDAQ:VNOM) recently closed on its dropdown transaction from Diamondback Energy (FANG). It also closed on a smaller acquisition in February.

These deals are expected to result in Viper's oil production averaging around 48,000 barrels per day during the rest of 2025, up from under 30,000 barrels of oil per day in Q4 2024.

I had modeled a potential dropdown transaction before, and the actual dropdown deal was pretty close to what I expected.

I now estimate Viper's value at $43.50 to $46 per share at long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil. This is with a somewhat conservative approach to future production, as it is based on 48,000 barrels per day of oil production, while Viper had noted that the dropdown transaction assets are expected to grow oil production by 3,000 barrels per day from Diamondback-operated acreage.

Thus there is a decent chance of 2026 production being above 48,000 barrels of oil per day, even if other operators trim their development plans. Diamondback has been prioritizing development at areas where Viper has higher NRIs.

Recent Transactions

Viper announced at the beginning of May that it had closed the previously announced dropdown transaction with Diamondback Energy. This dropdown transaction involved Viper issuing approximately 69.6 million OpCo units (with the same amount of Class B shares) and paying $1 billion in cash to Diamondback. The deal was valued at $4.45 billion based on Viper's unit price during the 30 trading days prior to the announcement.

Viper previously closed (in February) its $330 million Morita Ranches acquisition, where it acquired 1,691 net royalty acres in Howard County for $211 million in cash and approximately 2.4 million OpCo units (and Class B shares).

The two acquisitions have a combined value of $4.78 billion, involving approximately 72 million OpCo units/Class B shares and $1.21 billion in cash.

Viper issued approximately 28.336 million Class A shares at $44.50 per share in a February 2025 public offering. This resulted in approximately $1.2 billion in net proceeds for Viper, which basically covers the cash consideration for its acquisitions.

Viper received around 23,100 net royalty acres in the Midland Basin and approximately 1,700 net royalty acres in the Delaware and Williston Basins (combined) from these deals.

The average NRI is 5.0% for the Diamondback-operated acreage in the Midland Basin (which is over 16,000 net royalty acres).

The expected 2025 production from the (combined) acquired assets is 32,000 BOEPD, including around 18,000 barrels of oil per day. Viper also expects Diamondback-operated oil production to increase by 3,000 barrels per day from 2025 to 2026. These assets would have oil production of 21,000 barrels per day in 2026 with flat growth from other operators.

Post Drop Down Transaction Results

I last looked at Viper before it announced the two transactions, but had modeled out a scenario where it made a $4.2 billion transaction that brought its production up to 85,500 BOEPD, including 48,500 barrels per day of oil production.

Viper's guidance for the remainder of 2025 (after the closing of its Diamondback dropdown transaction) is for around 86,500 BOEPD in average production, including 48,000 barrels per day of oil production.

This is close to what I had modeled before, albeit with slightly higher total production and a 1% lower oil cut.

At those production levels and $60 WTI oil (roughly current strip over the next year), Viper would generate approximately $1.295 billion in revenues inclusive of hedges.

This doesn't include lease bonus income, which can be pretty lumpy. I've assumed an average of $70 million per year in lease bonus income over the long-term, but Viper may go extended periods with only a minor amount of lease bonus income.

Viper is thus projected to end up with around $960 million in distributable cash flow during the next year at $60 WTI oil. This is approximately $3.33 per share in distributable cash flow.

$ Million

Updated Valuation

I have moved to a $70 WTI long-term oil price to value companies now. At long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, I now estimate Viper's value at $43.50 to $46 per share.

This includes the effect of lower oil prices until the end of 2026 and is based on its post-dropdown transaction production of 86,500 BOEPD in total production, with 48,000 barrels per day of oil production. I've also assumed an average of $70 million per year in lease bonus income over the long-term.

Giving Viper credit for the 3,000 additional barrels of oil production per day expected next year for the Diamondback-operated acreage in its recent dropdown transaction would add another $2.50 per share in value (bringing it up to around $46 to $48.50 per share).

I generally prefer to be more conservative though, so I'm using the 48,000 barrels of oil per day number as my base case scenario.

Conclusion

Viper Energy is increasing its oil production by over 60% from Q4 2024 levels with its recent deals. Viper's share count ended up going up by a bit over 50% since late 2024, so its oil production per share is increasing by mid-single digits percent.

At $60 WTI oil, Viper's distributable cash flow may be around $3.33 per share over the next year without any lease bonus income. At my long-term price of $70 WTI oil and with $70 million per year in lease bonus income, Viper may be able to generate over $4 per share in distributable cash flow.

At long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil, I have updated my estimate of Viper's value to around $43.50 to $46 per share.

data.title: Ross Stores: Buy The Dip Despite Tariff Impact
data.description:
Ross Stores (NASDAQ:ROST) operates 2,186 stores selling off-price apparel and home fashion products in the US market. Ross Stores posted a flat same store sales growth (SSS) in Q1 FY25 accompanied by a weak Q2 guidance. Despite the near-term challenges, I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $160 per share.

Ross Stores posted its Q1 FY25 result on May 22, delivering flat SSS growth as depicted in the chart below. Importantly, the management noted that half of their products sold originated in China and the tariff duties could significantly impact their business growth in the near term.

It is evident that their SSS was sluggish in the current quarter, primarily due to weak consumer confidence and challenges from high interest rates and tariffs. In addition, the apparel and home-related merchandise are somewhat discretionary, and consumers might defer purchases if they have tight cash flow. As such, I anticipate Ross Stores’s growth will continue facing challenges in FY25.

As detailed in the table below, Ross Stores has around 26% of revenue exposure in home accents, bed and bath categories, which are more sensitive to the overall consumption environment, in my view.

Historically, Ross Stores has demonstrated its capability to grow its SSS by 3%-5% due to its compelling off-price strategy and a variety of merchandise, favored by family customers.

In addition, Ross Stores has a very consistent capital allocation policy. They generated $4.4 billion in free cash flow over the past three years, distributed $1.37 billion in dividends and repurchased almost $3 billion of own shares. As such, they almost returned all the free cash flow to the shareholders via dividends and stock repurchase, which is a very generous capital allocation policy, in my view.

The company is guiding for 0%-3% same store sales growth for Q2 FY25, and the tariff is expected to create $0.11 to $0.16 of headwinds to the EPS. During the call, the management noted that the tariff will reduce its operating margin by 90-120bps for the second quarter. The TJX Companies (TJX), Ross Stores’s competitor, reported its Q1 FY26 earnings on May 21; however, they maintained its guidance despite tariff impacts. This suggest that Ross Stores does not manage its supply chain and cost initiatives as effectively as TJX, which disappointed the market after the earning release.

Due to the high interest rates and weak consumption, I anticipate Ross Stores will deliver 3% SSS growth in FY25, a lower-than-normal rate compared with historical average. In terms of store expansion, the company’s total number of stores increased by 4.7% in FY23 and 3.7% in FY24. During the call, the management highlighted their plan to open 90 new stores this year. As such, I forecast new stores growth will contribute an additional 2.5% growth to the topline.

From FY26 onwards, I anticipate the interest rate, and overall consumption will start to normalize. As such, I forecast the company’s SSS will recover to 5% from FY26, and the total revenue will grow by 7.5% after factoring in new store growth.

During the call, the management anticipated the merchandise margin will be similar to the prior year excluding tariff impact. As such, I estimate the operating margin will decline by 20bps in FY25 due to tariff impact. From FY26, I forecast 10bps annual margin expansion driven by operation efficiencies and leverage from SG&A expenses, aligned with the historical trend.

With these assumptions, the DCF can be summarized as follows:

FCFF:

The WACC is calculated to be 9.4% assuming: risk free 4.2%; beta 0.9; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $50 Bn, debt $2.2 billion; tax rate 24%. I set the terminal growth to 4%, aligned with the overall consumption growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting the net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $160 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

  • The off-price retail market is highly competitive, and entry barriers are not that high. Specifically, Ross Stores has some notable competitors such as TJX (TJX). The competitive landscape forces Ross Stores to continually improve its customer service, product sourcing capabilities and overall operational efficiencies.
  • As disclosed in its 10K, the company has pending litigations related to violations of wage and hour laws in California. As reported by the media, Ross Stores laid off more than 1,600 employees in its distribution centers in California. As the legal proceedings are still in the process, the potential outcome remains unknown at this moment.
  • Lastly, I think the tariff remains the biggest risk for the company, as its off-price business model is dependent on the low sourcing costs from brand merchandisers. If the tariff raised the overall costs for apparel and home products, Ross Stores might face significant challenges to keep their price low to attract their target customers.

Conclusion

While Ross Stores delivered a disappointed quarterly result, I think their long-term growth remains intact. I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $160 per share.

data.title: Kohl's: Low Valuation, High Uncertainty
data.description:
Kohl's Corporation (NYSE:KSS) has been trading down after the initial post pandemic rally on multiple counts. The top line has been pressured by weaker spending trends as well as KSS' brand positioning issues, and a bottom is not yet in sight. On the other hand, some signs of the turnaround initiatives paying off are visible in the bounce back of gross margins in recent quarters. However, the story looks more bearish as investors wait for clearer strategic direction to re-rate the stock.

Brand Struggles and Turnaround Strategy

The rise of e-commerce and off-price retail has been a known pushback for departmental stores. KSS still persists with legacy large format outlet and off-mall locations, which is at loggerheads with its pivot to smaller convenience-based outlets. The smaller stores too have not been able to pull back overall performance metrics, adding to the problem, and perhaps pointing to brand identity issues.

Kohl's is facing a brand identity crisis, trapped between the premium and discount markets. Traditionally serving middle income shoppers with quality products, Kohl's shift from private labels to premium market partnerships has alienated loyal customers without attracting new ones. Efforts to modernize through trendy collaborations and celebrity endorsements have yielded mixed results, leaving the brand identity unclear. Value-driven shoppers increasingly favor off-price retailers, challenging Kohl's relevance. Since 2023, Kohl's has focused on store refreshes, tighter inventory control, and expanding Sephora to over 850 stores, boosting foot traffic and basket size. While margins have improved, topline growth remains weak, with the company still facing fatigue in revenue gains despite these turnaround initiatives.

Dividend Risk

Kohl's dividend safety is questionable due to structural financial strain. With a dividend coverage ratio of just 0.39 and interest coverage at 1.60, the company is generating insufficient earnings to comfortably support both debt servicing and dividend payouts. This pressure is worsened by a cash dividend payout ratio of 122% and a free cash flow yield to dividend yield of only 0.82%, meaning dividends are being funded beyond the company's cash-generating capacity. While its historically high yield is currently supporting investor interest, the dividend factor is eroding fast as payout ratios exceeding 200% signal an unsustainable path and likely further cuts (after the significant cut in the past year).

Valuation Depressed on Structural Concerns

KSS' forward EV to EBITDA shows discount valuations compared to bigger retail players like Target and Nordstrom, and even to Macy's. The broader valuation trends also point to struggles in the sector and an upcoming macro headwind risk from decreased discretionary spending. KSS positions itself in the mid-income segment and on non-discretionary purchases, which is a potential reason why its valuations will be pressured (as are the other peers') due to broader sectoral pressures.

The relative valuation, and KSS' own discount relative to history may look like a value buy now, and it could be, provided the margin bounce back of the past few quarters is complemented by a turnaround in revenues. In Q4 FY 2024, comparable sales declined by 6.7%. This is not a great sign of the brand regaining its foothold. Although it is lower than the net sales drop of 9.4%, the recent store closures could be impacting the overall sales numbers and not the comps.

Profitability also took a significant hit as operating income dropped by more than half to $126m YoY, pushing operating margins down to 2.3%. Net income and adjusted EPS also saw sharp declines, reflecting pressures on both top and bottom lines. Management's 2025 outlook remains cautious, projecting further sales declines of 5-7% and EPS between $0.10 and $0.60, signaling ongoing structural challenges at KSS as well as the retail environment overall.

Difficult to Sell at These Valuations

Despite all the concerns, KSS is trading at historical lows with significant short interest (~50%). Shorting at these levels is very open to short squeeze risks, even without re-rating triggers.

On the other hand, a buy is not warranted until turnaround in top line and bottom line are evident, which is unlikely in the next year, given KSS' own conservative guidance and challenging macro headwinds. Even the lucrative dividend yield is at risk, preventing any supporting buy thesis. My overall rating for KSS is therefore a Hold, with re-rating triggers to be monitored for exits or fresh entries.

The management's guidance for Q1 2025 is a 4-4.3% drop in comparable sales and an EPS in the range of $0.2-$0.24. Any improvement beyond expectations could be a re-rating trigger. Developments from KSS's turnaround strategies on partnerships (like Sephora) or product assortment adjustments, will also be a support for KSS shares. Margin uptrends and positive guidance could also push the share prices up.

In the upcoming earnings report, I will look at the comparable sales to see whether there are any signals of stabilizing demand. An improvement from the last quarter towards the management guidance is also good to show progress, though not an entry point. I will also look at gross and operating margins for any signs of stabilization or improvement, indicating better cost control. Updates on strategic initiatives such as brand revitalization, customer experience enhancements, or progress in partnerships will also be crucial.

data.title: Redwood Trust Senior Notes: High Slippage For This Baby Bond (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We covered the 9% Senior Notes from Redwood (NYSE:RWTO) last year with a 'Buy' rating. We own the name and closely watching it as well. Since our rating, the total return on the position is positive (i.e. when you factor in the dividends), but the bond price has moved lower.

In today's article we are going to analyze the latest developments with the parent company Redwood Trust (RWT), and highlight why we are downgrading the name to 'Hold'.

High Drawdown During the April 2025 Market Sell-off

One aspect which surprised us was the high drawdown for the bonds during the recent market sell-off, especially when compared to peers:

Both RWTO and RWTN (the other baby bond from Redwood), suffered drawdowns close to -7% during the recent market turmoil. As we can see from the above graph, the two bonds were by far the laggards, only followed to a certain extent by AOMN, a baby bond from Angel Oak. Spread duration is not a massive factor here, with RWTO sporting a 4.3 years implied spread duration, while CIMN has a 4-year one. CIMN however, only suffered a -3.5% drawdown.

The market is perceiving RWT as a much higher risk entity, therefore the required yields on its debentures increased. Let us have a closer look at RWT's latest results and fundamental indicators.

Redwood Trust Fundamental Performance - Stable Book Value

As per its Q1 2025 financials, RWT maintained a stable book value:

GAAP book value per common share was $8.39 at March 31, 2025, relative to $8.46 per share at December 31, 2024

For the REIT world, book value is very important because it speaks about the ability of an entity to be an ongoing one and create value and economic returns for shareholders. The company also maintains a strong dividend coverage, with earnings available for distribution (a non-GAAP metric) increasing:

The increase in EAD per share simply means that the underlying assets are delivering cash flows to the business. Also, to note the company is not as leveraged as some mREITs, with recourse leverage at only 2.5x:

Many times, non-recourse leverage pertains to securitizations, when the company retains an equity or mezz tranche. Securitizations are considered term funding for equity holders, and are non-recourse because the collateral is usually ring-fenced and the issuer does not pledge any other assets.

From a net income standpoint, the company offered a positive surprise, with a larger figure than last year:

The Net Income figure was $14,397,000, higher than Q4 2025. To note that most of the difference came from lower negative changes in the fair value of its investments. The company has residential consumer and investor loans on its books:

Also, please note that most of the funding is done via 'ABS issued' as liabilities. In effect, RWT has three main segments, with only one involving putting significant assets on the balance sheet:

Residential Mortgage Banking: Acquires residential loans from third-party originators for sale, securitization, or transfer to its investment portfolio, often through its Sequoia (SEMT) private-label securitization program. It also uses derivative financial instruments to manage associated risks.

Business Purpose Mortgage Banking: Through its CoreVest platform, it originates loans for residential investors (e.g., single-family and multifamily properties) and bridge loans, which are then securitized, sold, or held in its investment portfolio.

Investment Portfolio: Invests in assets like mortgage-backed securities, business purpose lending bridge loans, and other housing-related investments, including Freddie Mac K-Series multifamily loan securitizations and home equity investments.

The assets on the balance sheet are sensitive to interest rates, with higher rates translating into lower values. Hence, the rise in rates this year has put pressure on the common equity.

The picture we are getting from the latest financials is one of a stable-business which is well-managed, one which covers its dividend but is subject to risks from higher rates. We can clearly see that historically from the common equity price:

Please note the +25% rally from May 2024 to October 2024 when yields started moving lower before the first Fed rate cut. Both the common shares and the underlying net income are affected by rates as we have seen. Higher rates will put pressure on the balance sheet.

Risk Factors - Higher Rates and a Housing Meltdown

Higher rates represent the main risk factor for both the common equity and the baby bonds. We have seen how higher rates hit the assets on the balance sheet for the company, which subsequently percolates down to the common equity. Higher rates also affect the baby bonds via a higher discount factor. This is very simple bond math, with RWTO going down in value when 5-year rates move higher. A continued pressure on yields from the budget deficit and failure to cut spending can keep negatively affecting Redwood and RWTO.

The second risk factor here is a recession that can cause a housing meltdown. The company has on its balance sheet or via its securitizations exposure to residential loans:

While many of the loans are seasoned with an acceptable loan-to-value, a housing crisis can increase delinquencies and foreclosures. We can see delinquencies ticking-up across all types of loans, with the worst increase seen in CAFL loans. If increased delinquencies do result in foreclosures, then the market price of the property matters since it will need to be sold. Currently, we are seeing these levels without a recession materializing. The market is therefore concerned in a risk-off environment that a recession can create true losses for Redwood if homeowners default and the market value of the property is below its loan value.

Conclusion

RWTO is a baby bond from Redwood Trust. We first started covering the debenture last year when we actually purchased it. Although RWTO is now trading below par, we are downgrading the name to 'Hold' due to the performance of the bonds during the recent market sell-off and the high sensitivity of both the company and bonds to higher interest rates. While other REITs such as PennyMac (PMT) have mortgage servicing rights on their balance sheet to attenuate the impact of higher rates, RWT does not. We are holding the bonds clipping the coupon but will not add until we get some macro clarity regarding the ultimate path of long rates.
